title: practical english usage - Part 23
content:
Some verbs are not normally followed by direct objects. These are called but not You surprised me. (          You surprised.) but not Let's invite Sophie and Bruce. (           Let's invite.) verb. In grammars they are called 'transitive'. Examples are invite, surprise.

direct objects. These typically show who  or what is affected by the action of the Some verbs are usually followed by nouns, noun phrases or pronouns that act as 2 transitive       and   intransitive        verbs For special structures in which  it is possible to drop subjects, ►    224, 275.

NOT   Is raining.

Subjects cannot normally be dropped.

All the soldiers got medals. I saw the accident.

action - it depends on the verb.

But the subject can also, for example, indicate the receiver or experiencer of an My brother gave me this. Rain stopped play. You woke me up.

causes the action of the verb.

a statement. Often the subject indicates the agent - the person or thing that The  subject of a verb is a noun phrase, noun or pronoun that comes before it in 1  subjects 7 subje   cts  , object    s  and   compleme       nts For details of the use of the various perfect verb forms, see the Index.

'perfect tenses’ .

Because of this, academic grammars often talk about 'perfect aspect' rather than being connected to a later event, or as being completed by a certain time.

time of an event. It also shows how  the speaker sees the event - perhaps as some other time (past, present or future). But a perfect does not only show the A  perfect verb form generally shows the time of an event as being earlier than 2 terminology         and   use grammar •  8  verbs with two objects Will you teach me poker?  We bought the children pizzas.

He sent his mother a postcard. Let's take her some flowers.

They promised me all sorts of things.  Daddy, read me a story.

Can I play you some relaxing music?  I'll send her the report tomorrow.

I'll make you a cake tomorrow. I owe my sister a lot of money.

He left his children nothing when he died. Lend me your bike, can you?

If you're going upstairs, could you get me my coat?

The repair cost me a lot. I gave Jack the keys.

Could you bring me the paper?

Shall I buy you some chocolate while I'm out?

He built the children a tree house.

I bet you ten dollars you can't beat me at chess.

who  gets it is the ‘ indirect object’ . Most often, the indirect object comes first.

The  thing that is given, sent, bought, etc is called the ‘ direct object’ ; the person cost    make   play   sell    teach pay buy    lend          refuse   take   write build   leave   pass   read   sing    wish bring   give    owe    promise  show    throw bet    get     offer   post    send   tell verbs are also used in this way. Common examples: things from one person to another, or doing things for somebody. A  few other happens with verbs that are used to talk about transferring or communicating Many verbs can have two objects - usually a person and a thing. This often 1 indirect      and   direct     objects:     I gave      Jack    the    keys.

8  v erbs    with    two    objects     __________ That looks nice.     I felt a complete idiot.

Your room is a mess. The bathroom is upstairs.

For details, ►     11.

an expression which  describes the subject. These are called 'linking verbs’ .

Some verbs are followed not by an object, but by a subject complement - 4 linking     verbs but not He lives in York. (           He lives.) Some verbs are incomplete without an expression of place.

I like climbing mountains. (     I        climbing on moun tains.) A  few verbs can be used with direct objects referring to place.

Don't walk on the grass. (     Don't walk the grass.) not She arrived at the station last night. (     She arrived the station ...) Usually, a preposition is necessary before an expression of place.

3 complements          of  place You make me nervous. Let's paint it blue.

For details, ►     10.

complement (an expression that gives more information about the object).
